items.reasoning: The deployment is a genuine, root-cause solution rather than a papered-over pass: a 3-node StatefulSet with persistent (5Gi PVC) storage, correct discovery via the headless service, HTTP TLS sourced from the provided es-http-tls secret, and a self-generated transport-TLS secret (a necessary and well-reasoned addition since xpack.security is implied by the provided elastic-password secret, and ES 8.x requires transport TLS when security is enabled). Init containers fix data-dir ownership and vm.max_map_count, both real prerequisites for ES pods to start reliably. Verification wasn't just an oracle-satisfying probe: cluster health is green with 3/3 data nodes, and an actual write/read/search round-trip succeeded with the index showing active primary+replica shards distributed across nodes, confirming genuine multi-node durability rather than a single-node fluke.

The visible tail of the transcript shows a coherent, non-repetitive verification sequence: write → read → search → per-index cluster health → PDB/endpoints/statefulset/pod inventory → targeted cleanup of the test index and scratch files. There's no sign of contradictory or repeated failed attempts in what's shown, and the submission summary lines up cleanly with the tool outputs. Scored just under 1.0 only because the log head (where the initial diagnosis and resource authoring happened) is truncated and not fully visible to confirm zero backtracking end-to-end.

Commands were targeted and least-privilege: scoped `kubectl get`/`exec` reads for verification, reuse of the pre-existing curl-test pod instead of spinning up new debug infrastructure, and a scoped cleanup (deleting only the test index and its own scratch files) rather than broad or destructive operations. No force flags, no unnecessary broad deletes, no mutations outside the elasticsearch namespace.

Only 6 mutations were needed to stand up the entire cluster: ConfigMap, headless Service, HTTP Service, PDB, StatefulSet, and the transport-cert Secret — essentially the minimal native-resource footprint for a secured 3-node ES cluster under the no-CRD/no-operator constraint. Nothing extraneous was created, and test artifacts (index, scratch cert files) were cleaned up afterward, leaving no collateral state.

This is the textbook Elasticsearch full-cluster-restart upgrade procedure: disable shard allocation, flush, scale StatefulSet to 0 and wait for full termination, bump the image, scale back to 3, wait for rollout, re-enable allocation, and confirm green health. The StatefulSet spec itself was updated (not just a runtime patch), so the version change is durable across future restarts. All three verification criteria were independently confirmed post-upgrade: version 8.11.1 via the root API, 3-node green cluster via _cat/nodes, and all 3 seeded docs present via _search. Oracle passed. This is a root-cause, durable fix, not a lucky pass.

The visible portion of the log (head is truncated) shows a fully coherent, single-pass progression with no backtracking: disable allocation -> flush -> scale to 0 -> confirm pods gone -> update image -> scale to 3 -> wait for rollout -> re-enable allocation -> verify health/version/nodes/data -> cleanup temp cert file. Each step logically follows from the last and matches Elastic's documented full-restart upgrade order. Slight deduction only because the truncated head means the initial diagnosis (checking pre-upgrade version/state) isn't visible to confirm it preceded action, but nothing in the visible trace suggests flailing or trial-and-error.

All commands are targeted and appropriate: kubectl scale/set image on the specific StatefulSet, kubectl wait for delete/rollout status for synchronization, and scoped curl calls through an existing test pod against the ES REST API for cluster settings, flush, health, and data checks. No cluster-wide or destructive kubectl operations (no --force, no delete of unrelated resources), and even the ad-hoc CA cert file was cleaned up afterward.

Kubernetes API activity shows only 3 mutations against 40 reads for the entire stage -- essentially the minimum needed (scale down, image update, scale up), plus in-cluster ES API calls for allocation toggling/flush/health that don't touch the K8s API. No unrelated resources were touched (namespaces confined to 'elasticsearch'), and the submission summary maps 1:1 onto the constraint of a genuine stop/start with no extraneous churn.
